sources: ILOSTAT 2024 — wage floors and sector growth indices
World Bank WDI 2024 — gross secondary enrollment
Frey & Osborne (2017) — automation probability scores, LMIC-adjusted by ILO (2019)
ILO Future of Work task-content indices (2020)
Wittgenstein Centre for Demography — Education Projections 2025-2035
World Bank STEP Skills Measurement Programme
_meta.note: All econometric signals are configurable here without changing application code.

education_taxonomy.name: NVTI / CTVET
education_taxonomy.full_name: National Vocational Training Institute / Council for Technical and Vocational Education and Training

gender_wage_gap.note: Women in Ghana earn approximately 18% less than men in comparable informal economy roles.

wittgenstein_projections.narrative: Secondary enrollment in Ghana is projected to rise ~11 percentage points by 2035, increasing competition for mid-skill formal roles. Workers without documented credentials will face growing disadvantage.
wittgenstein_projections.note: IoT-adjacent and solar-tech demand grows 4.1%/yr through 2035 in Greater Accra; current TVET supply meets ~22% of demand.

frey_osborne_lmic_calibration.calibration_note: Automation probability in LMIC contexts is 15-25% lower than OECD baseline due to lower capital-labour cost ratios and higher demand for relational/physical tasks in informal markets.

policy_insights: Northern districts show 2× higher skill gaps than coastal — prioritise mobile NVTI units in Wa & Bolgatanga.
Phone-repair informal labour overlaps 71% with ESCO 7421 — recognising prior learning could formalise ~12,400 youth in 18 months.
Wittgenstein 2025-2035 (SSP2): IoT-adjacent demand grows 4.1%/yr through 2035 in Greater Accra; current TVET supply meets ~22%.
ILO LMIC calibration: automation risk in Ghana's informal sector is 18% lower than OECD baseline — wage competition, not displacement, is the primary risk.
World Bank HCI 0.45 — closing the skills-recognition gap by 10pp could add 0.8% to annual GDP per World Bank STEP modelling.
gh.sample_narrative: I've run a phone repair business since I was 17 in Madina market. I fix screens, batteries, and sometimes I help my cousin sell SIM cards.

education_taxonomy.name: NAVTTC / TEVTA
education_taxonomy.full_name: National Vocational and Technical Training Commission / Technical Education and Vocational Training Authority

gender_wage_gap.note: Women in Pakistan earn approximately 34% less than men in comparable roles, one of the largest gender wage gaps in South Asia.

wittgenstein_projections.narrative: Secondary enrollment in Pakistan is projected to rise ~14 percentage points by 2035 under SSP2. More credentialed workers entering the market makes undocumented skills harder to prove without a formal record.
wittgenstein_projections.note: Solar-installation demand grows 5.7%/yr in Punjab through 2035; current NAVTTC throughput covers 18% of projected need.

frey_osborne_lmic_calibration.calibration_note: Pakistan's rural-agricultural context reduces automation risk by 20-28% vs OECD baseline. Physical, relational, and context-dependent tasks dominate informal markets.

policy_insights: Balochistan & southern Punjab show critical gaps — TEVTA mobile training pods could reach ~8,900 youth in Quetta corridor.
Informal mobile-repair workforce maps to ESCO 7421 with 68% confidence — RPL pathways exist but uptake is <4%.
Wittgenstein 2025-2035 (SSP2): solar-installation demand grows 5.7%/yr in Punjab; current NAVTTC throughput covers 18% of projected need.
World Bank STEP data: workers with recognized vocational credentials earn 23% more than unrecognized peers with equivalent skills.
ILO LMIC calibration: 71.5% of Pakistan's workforce is informal — automation displaces tasks, not workers, in this context; upskilling outperforms job-matching alone.
pk.sample_narrative: I've been repairing mobile phones in my uncle's shop in Anarkali since I was 16. I do screens, batteries, software flashing, and small motherboard work.
